OpenAI is pushing for an IPO in a turbulent market. Most commentary focuses on the financials. But the more interesting question is about timing.

THE TIMING PARADOX
OpenAI has the most users, the most revenue ($25B ARR), and the highest valuation ($852B) in AI. So why not wait for better market conditions?

Because their internal clocks are moving faster than the market's.

FOUR STRATEGIC PRESSURES
1. Talent mobility is high across the AI industry.
 OpenAI has seen significant leadership transitions — and in a market where every major company is hiring AI talent aggressively, the equity liquidity promise matters. An IPO converts paper wealth into real retention.

2. The competitive landscape is shifting fast.
 Enterprise API share has moved from 50% (2023) to 25% today. Anthropic and Google have grown significantly. The "clear market leader" positioning is strongest right now — it gets harder to claim with each quarter.

3. The cost structure is uniquely challenging.
 Unlike traditional SaaS, AI inference costs scale with usage. At 57 cents cost per dollar earned, the path to profitability requires either cheaper compute (custom chips, efficient architectures) or significantly higher margins on enterprise contracts. Both take time.

4. Legal complexity adds urgency.
 The ongoing non-profit conversion proceedings create uncertainty that's easier to navigate as a public company with established governance than as a private company mid-transition.

THE SYSTEMS INSIGHT
What makes this interesting from a strategy perspective: Each pressure reinforces the others.Talent retention depends on equity value. Equity value depends on market position. Market position depends on model quality.
Model quality depends on talent. When a stock's outflow weakens its own inflow, you get a self-reinforcing dynamic. The longer you wait, the harder each problem becomes. 

This is why the IPO feels urgent despite the market. It's not about the best time to go public. It's about the window of time where the story still holds together.

Navigating the New Reality of ITES & Outsourcing / Captive Job Market

The ITES / Outsourcing industry is no longer what it used to be—and that’s not a bad thing.

Over the last couple of years, we’ve seen a steady recovery in hiring, with sectors like ITES showing renewed momentum and even double-digit growth toward the end of 2025.  At the same time, the industry itself is expanding rapidly, driven by AI, cloud, automation, and global outsourcing demand.

But here’s the reality job seekers must acknowledge:

The nature of jobs is changing faster than the number of jobs.

AI and automation are reshaping roles—from customer support to backend operations—making some traditional roles redundant while creating entirely new opportunities.

Nearly 40% of the workforce needs upskilling to stay relevant.

Employers are increasingly looking for skills over degrees.

So, what does this mean for job seekers?

Upskill continuously
Focus on areas like AI tools, data handling, cloud basics, cybersecurity, and digital communication.

Build human-centric strengths
Empathy, problem-solving, and stakeholder management are becoming more valuable as automation rises.

Be open to new formats of work
Gig roles, contract opportunities, and project-based work are becoming the new normal.

Adopt a “learn–unlearn–relearn” mindset
The industry rewards adaptability more than experience alone.

Don’t wait to be job-ready—stay job-ready
Consistency beats intensity in today’s market.

The ITES/BPO industry still holds massive potential—with billions in growth and increasing global demand—but success will belong to those who evolve with it, not resist it.

Before You Hire, Automate!

Before you post that job listing, ask yourself one question.
Is this a people problem or a process problem?

Most service businesses hit a ceiling and immediately think "we need more hands." Sometimes that's true. But more often than not, half the work burying your team shouldn't need a human at all.

Here are 5 automations we build for clients before they even think about hiring:

1. Lead Follow-Up. A lead fills out your form at 11pm. Nobody replies until 10am. By then they've already talked to your competitor. Automated qualification + instant response fixes that overnight.

2. Client Onboarding Contracts, welcome emails, intake forms, internal task creation, Slack notifications to the team. All triggered the moment a deal closes. No one has to remember anything.

3. Proposals and Invoices. Still copying and pasting from old templates? One trigger pulls client data, generates the doc, sends it for signature, and follows up if it's not signed in 48 hours.

4. Past Client Re-engagement. You have hundreds of old clients sitting in your CRM collecting dust. A simple reactivation sequence can turn dormant contacts into repeat revenue without lifting a finger.

5. Internal Reporting. Your team spends hours pulling numbers into spreadsheets every week. Automated dashboards update in real time and notify you when something needs attention.

Each one of these replaces 5 to 10 hours of manual work per week. Stack all five and you've just freed up a full-time employee's worth of capacity.

Your next hire should do work only a human can do. Not work a workflow should've handled months ago.

That's what we build at Baraco.ai. Let's audit your ops before you open that job listing.

Most B2B companies are still running ABM like it's 2021.
Pick 50 logos from a Sheet. Upload to LinkedIn. Run display ads. Send a templated sequence. Wait for a form fill that never comes.

Then the quarter ends and everyone blames "the market".

Here's how we actually run it in the AI-native era.

1. Top of funnel is no longer a list. It's a live feed, static TALs refreshed quarterly are dead. and now it's signals: hiring moves, funding, tech stack shifts, product launches, 10-K mentions, and how your brand shows up inside ChatGPT and Perplexity answers.

AI agents monitor this 24/7. Accounts move in and out of priority weekly.
If your TAL looks the same as three months ago, you're not doing ABM. You're doing a spreadsheet.

2. Research is the new creative. "Personalization at scale" is mostly first name tokens in a paragraph that could've been sent to anyone.

Real personalization in 2026:
A 2 page POV doc built for one account, based on their last earnings call, roadmap, and the competitors they're losing deals to, this delivered to 4-5 people in the committee, each with a different angle. 

If your SDR can't explain in 2 sentences why this account should care THIS QUARTER, don't reach out yet.

3. Middle of funnel is where most teams die.
You got the meeting. Now what?

Most teams hand it to sales and marketing disappears. Big mistake. This is 
where buying committees form and deals get stuck.
What works:

One shared account view between sales, marketing, and CS. Followed by account specific microsites built in hours, not weeks and content mapped to the committee, not the persona. 

4. Bottom of funnel is about momentum, not closing.
Everyone obsesses over close rate. The real metric is velocity.
How fast does an account go from first signal to signed contract?
Track buying committee coverage, engagement depth, and multi-channel touchpoints. 

5. Kill the vanity metrics.

ABM in the AI-native era is not about doing more. It's about doing less, with ten times the depth. Smaller lists. Deeper research. Tighter alignment. Faster signal response.

🚨 Designing an end-to-end technology workforce for the AI-first era

Very few are talking about the real disruption: the workforce itself.
After reading this piece from McKinsey & Company (link in comments), one thing is clear:
👉 AI isn’t just changing what we build
👉 It’s changing how organizations are designed
And most companies are not ready.

⚠️ The uncomfortable reality CIOs are facing today:
• You’re expected to cut costs AND deliver more innovation
• You must scale globally while managing geopolitical risk
• Vendors promise AI value… but ROI is unclear
• You need new AI talent, while reskilling your entire workforce
That’s not transformation.
That’s a tightrope.
(The article calls out these four simultaneous pressures explicitly )

🧠 The deeper problem
Most organizations are still:
Hiring for roles, not skills
Measuring vendors on effort, not outcomes
Treating AI as a tool, not a teammate
Meanwhile…
AI is already:
Replacing routine development work
Amplifying top talent disproportionately
Forcing a rethink of what “a team” even means

🔄 What needs to change (and fast)
💡 1. Hire for judgment, not capacity
Fewer people. Higher impact.
AI handles execution—humans define direction.
💡 2. Build capabilities AI can’t replace
Not everything should be outsourced.
Owning architecture, data, and product thinking is now critical.
💡 3. Treat vendors as value partners
Shift from “hours billed” → outcomes delivered
AI makes old contracts obsolete.
💡 4. Move to human + agent teams
This is the biggest mindset shift:
Employees won’t just use AI—they’ll manage it.

⚡ The hard truth
This isn’t a tech transformation.
It’s a change management challenge at scale.
And most companies are underestimating it.

🔥 The takeaway
The winners in the AI era won’t be the ones who adopt AI fastest…
They’ll be the ones who redesign their workforce deliberately:
Where human judgment sits
What capabilities stay in-house
How value is created across humans, agents, and partners
Because in an AI-first world:
👉 Hiring strategy
👉 Capability building
👉 Vendor strategy
…are no longer separate decisions.

The core challenge: most web-based office editors are slow. Sluggish when typing. Painful over any real-world connection. We don't measure output in lines of code. We use Claude, Cursor, and custom RAG pipelines as core infrastructure - not side tools. Day 65/100
"What are the major challenges regarding operational costs and margins in a supply side analysis of the IT sector?"

--> In supply side analysis, the IT sector faces a major problem where traditional high margin software models are being eroded by capital intensive infrastructure demands. While AI automates tasks, the underlying physical supply chain chips, data centres, and energy is becoming a massive operational burden that leaks profit before it reaches the bottom line.

1) The "Infrastructure Super Cycle" Squeeze

--> Surging Compute Costs : The average cost of computing is expected to climb 89% between 2023 and 2025, primarily driven by Generative AI.

--> Energy and Cooling Spikes : Unlike legacy software, AI hardware draws massive wattage. Powering a single high end GPU server can cost over $7,000 annually in electricity, with additional cooling setup costs ranging from $15,000 to $100,000.

--> Maintenance Tax : Ongoing maintenance for advanced AI hardware can consume 15-25% of the initial investment annually, covering everything from unplanned outages to model drift.

2) Transition from Labor Arbitrage to Asset Heavy

--> Revenue Effort Decoupling : For 30 years, IT services (especially in India) grew by hiring more people (labor arbitrage). AI increases productivity per employee, meaning projects that once needed 100 engineers now need far fewer, reducing billable hours and traditional revenue streams.

--> Margin Compression : Developing and integrating AI requires massive R&D spending that can shave 1-2% off near term operating margins. Firms that cannot monetize these high cost investments effectively risk obsolescence.

3) Supply Side "Margin Leakage"

--> Resource Underutilization : Many firms invest in expensive AI hardware but fail to keep it busy 24/7. If GPUs sit idle, the massive upfront capital investment is wasted.

--> The Talent Premium : A shortage of skilled AI professionals has spiked the cost of hiring and retaining top talent, inflating SG&A (selling, general, and administrative) expenses.

--> Data Silos : Cleaning and preparing fragmented data for AI can consume 25-40% of a total project cost, creating a hidden operational drain that is rarely priced into initial client contracts.

4) Pricing Power Challenges

--> Commoditization : As AI tools become standard, narrow service offerings are easily copied, leading to price wars and lower market pricing.

--> Inaccurate Estimations : Many IT firms still use legacy cost plus pricing models that fail to account for the high volatility of AI infrastructure and data storage costs, leading to loss making deals.

There is something almost Shakespearean about what is happening.

One of the most disruptive machines ever created by humankind now requires, at this early stage of enterprise deployment, more humans than ever to scale. OpenAI —the company that formally warned governments around the world that human labor will be progressively displaced by automation—is hiring at a rapid pace: architects, sales teams, pre-sales engineers, finance professionals, and technical consultants, effectively doubling its workforce.

Humans who will sell the end of human labor, without hypocrisy, as merely a transitional phase.

The real deployment of AI begins now. The era of basic chatbots and standard automations is over. We are talking about systems that read contracts of publicly listed companies, audit processes that affect the lives of millions, make decisions within complex approval workflows, and are held accountable.

To bridge the gap between a flashy demo and a mission-critical production system, you need someone who understands the concerns of a CEO, a CISO, or a CFO. You need someone who can speak about GDPR, financial statements, payroll structures, and prompt injection in the same meeting. You need humans.

Those humans are the automation consultants who endured the instability of platforms like Make and n8n, backend fragility with Supabase, constantly shifting API pricing, LLM models and products disappearing without notice, integrators working across everything with MCP, cybersecurity experts warning about vulnerabilities, and practitioners experimenting with open-source models on non-NVIDIA hardware due to scarcity. They are, without question, the resilient and underrecognized vanguard of this industrialization.

The system is reorganizing itself into three layers: those who build the models, those who provide the infrastructure, and those who know how to integrate these systems into the business model of a real organization. This third layer—AI consulting—will become the most valuable bottleneck, although in the not-so-distant future it too will be automatable, once decision-makers are willing to remove the last human who truly understands how AI was integrated across their entire enterprise.

The fiscal proposals put forward by OpenAI are not altruistic. When machines can independently perform what currently requires entire teams, someone will need to explain where the value has gone. And they would rather design that answer themselves before it is shaped by parliaments, labor unions, or public pressure.

Once again, Big Tech is setting the direction for democratically elected governments.

The Age of Intelligence is not here to free us from work. It claims it will redefine what kind of work is worthy of a human. And that definition will be the most important political battle of the coming decade.

AI should respect humans. Always. As the most important decision of our civilization.

The experimental phase of AI is officially over. 🌐

For the past five years, businesses have been "keeping an eye on it." In 2026, that window has closed. Regulators are issuing fines. Enterprise clients are demanding compliance certificates. And the gap between AI-ready businesses and AI-resistant ones is now wide enough to determine who survives the next business cycle.

Here are the 7 trends reshaping every business right now 👇

🤖 Trend 1: Agentic AI Moves From Demo to Deployment
AI that independently plans and executes multi-step tasks is now live on real operations floors — not just conference stages. The question is no longer "should we use it?" It is "do we have the governance to deploy it safely?"

⚖️ Trend 2: AI Audits Are Now Mandatory
Regulators are conducting their first formal AI audits. The most common failure point? Not broken technology — missing documentation. If it isn't written down, it is assumed non-compliant.

👥 Trend 3: AI in HR Faces Its First Legal Reckoning
AI used in hiring and performance reviews is now "High-Risk" under the EU AI Act. In the US, the EEOC has confirmed that discriminatory AI hiring outcomes violate civil rights law — regardless of intent. "The algorithm decided" is no longer a legal defense.

📊 Trend 4: Decision Intelligence Replaces Traditional Reporting
Static monthly reports are out. AI-powered dashboards that tell you what to do next — not just what happened last month — are in. Microsoft Copilot, Tableau AI, and Salesforce Einstein are leading this shift for businesses of every size.

🛡️ Trend 5: The AI Misinformation Arms Race Escalates
Deepfake CFO voice calls. AI-generated fake press releases. Synthetic executive video announcements. These are not theoretical — they are documented incidents happening to real companies right now.

🏪 Trend 6: AI for Small Business Goes From "Nice to Have" to "Must Have"
The price barrier is gone. A three-person agency can now access the same AI capabilities that once required an enterprise budget. The risk in 2026 is not moving too fast — it is moving too slowly.

🔍 Trend 7: Explainability Becomes a Board-Level Requirement
Insurance underwriters now require Explainable AI documentation as a condition of coverage. Enterprise clients include XAI compliance clauses in vendor contracts. "Black Box" AI is becoming commercially and legally unviable.

The businesses that win in 2026 are not the ones with the most advanced AI.
They are the ones with the most trusted AI. 🧭

I'm Sarah Brooks, and I help small businesses create workplaces where people thrive, and compliance isn't an afterthought. From crafting HR strategies to updating contracts, implementing policies, and building a culture of success, I make sure your people practices support your business goals.

If you want to avoid costly HR mistakes and set your business…

Stop using ChatGPT to write your resume.

Every hiring manager can spot it. And it's costing you interviews.

Right now, 𝟵𝟬% 𝗼𝗳 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝘀𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗼𝗻 𝗽𝗮𝗽𝗲𝗿.

You're not getting rejected. You're getting skipped.

Rejection means they saw you and said no. Skipping means you never existed to them.

I've reviewed 1𝟬𝟬+ 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗲𝘀 through the course of my career. Coached 𝟯𝟬+𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝘀 into offers.

Here's the pattern:

𝗔𝗜 𝘄𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘀𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱 𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱.
𝗛𝗶𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗵𝗶𝗿𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁𝘀.

The difference:

❌ 𝗗𝗘𝗔𝗗: "Collaborated with cross-functional teams to deliver projects"
→ Could be anyone
↳ No reason to stop

✅ 𝗔𝗟𝗜𝗩𝗘: "Led 6-person team across 3 departments, delivering $2.3M automation project 3 weeks early"

❌ 𝗗𝗘𝗔𝗗: "Improved processes and increased efficiency"
→ Sounds strong
↳ Says nothing

✅ 𝗔𝗟𝗜𝗩𝗘: "Redesigned workflow, cutting cycle time by 27% and saving 120+ hours/month"

❌ 𝗗𝗘𝗔𝗗: "Generated reports and analyzed data to support decision-making"
→ AI favorite
↳ No ownership

✅ 𝗔𝗟𝗜𝗩𝗘: "Built automated dashboard tracking 47 KPIs, reducing reporting time by 60%"

❌ 𝗗𝗘𝗔𝗗: "Worked with stakeholders to ensure project alignment"
→ Buzzwords
↳ No outcome

✅ 𝗔𝗟𝗜𝗩𝗘: "Managed 12 vendor contracts, driving $340K annual cost reduction"

❌ 𝗗𝗘𝗔𝗗: "Managed multiple projects in a fast-paced environment"
→ Everyone writes this

✅ 𝗔𝗟𝗜𝗩𝗘: "Delivered 4 concurrent projects worth $850K, all on time and 15% under budget"

See the shift?

From describing work… to proving impact.

That's what separates getting considered from getting skipped.

Because here's the truth most people miss:

𝗬𝗼𝘂'𝗿𝗲 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝗻 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝘆𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲.
𝗬𝗼𝘂'𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝗻 𝗰𝗹𝗮𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆.

And AI removes that when you rely on it blindly.

Here's how to use ChatGPT without killing your chances:

→ Use it for structure (60–70%)
↳ Clean language, better flow

→ Add YOUR proof (the 30% that gets you hired)
↳ Numbers
↳ Scope
↳ Business impact

→ Pressure test every line:
"Would 100 other engineers write this?"
↳ If yes → rewrite it

Your resume isn't there to sound professional.

It's there to make hiring you the obvious decision.

🗼 Tokyo’s cloud market is entering a very different phase.

For years, most conversations were around migration. Lift and shift, cost optimisation, basic modernisation.

Now it has changed.

The discussions I am having with clients today are more like:

💎 How do we scale infrastructure for AI driven workloads?
💎 How do we handle performance, latency, and resilience at scale?
💎 How do we find people who can actually build this?

Because the investment going into Japan right now is huge.

🪩 Amazon Web Services has committed over $15B into Japan’s cloud infrastructure.
🪩 Microsoft is investing another $10B while targeting 1M people trained in AI and cloud skills.

On the domestic side:

📍 SoftBank Group is building large scale AI and compute infrastructure.
📍 Nippon Telegraph and Telephone is pushing next generation data networks and energy efficient data centres.
📍 Fujitsu continues to expand hybrid and high performance computing capabilities.

At the same time, Japan’s data centre market is projected to reach around $38B by 2031.

So demand is not the issue.

Execution is.

There is still heavy reliance on legacy systems.
Power and land constraints are real, especially around Tokyo.
And the talent pool for cloud infrastructure at scale is tight.

Japan is moving, but it is moving carefully.

From a hiring perspective, the pressure points are clear:

👉 Cloud architects
👉 Platform and infrastructure engineers
👉 DevOps and SRE
👉 Cloud security and compliance

This is not early adoption anymore.
This is scale.

And scale creates urgency.

At Morgan McKinley, we support companies across Japan to scale their cloud teams, particularly through high impact contract talent when speed matters.

We also support urgent hiring when teams need to scale quickly or when there is a business critical gap that cannot wait.

As the FY26 annual reports roll out across the Indian B2B landscape this month, the market is quietly burying a five-year-old thesis: The "Discovery Premium."

For years, industrial aggregators raised massive rounds by simply digitizing the Rolodex. If you could build a platform that connected enterprise buyers with fragmented tier-3 suppliers, you were rewarded with a software-like valuation.

However, today, simply acting as a digital middleman is a fast track to severe valuation compression.

But the most critical takeaway from this earnings season isn't the death of the pure marketplace model, but it is the pivot.

The most formidable players in the space are abandoning the "asset-light" illusion and aggressively moving into digital contract manufacturing. They have realized that in major industrial hubs like the NCR, finding a supplier isn't the actual problem. The friction is guaranteeing the production quality, standardizing the defect rate, and ensuring strict delivery timelines.

The new, defensible moat is no longer just financing the transaction. It is taking absolute operational control of the supply chain physics.

What this forces is a complete teardown of how these organizations are built.

When we were brought in to consult with a few of the top brands in the aggregator space at Straffing AI last year, we warned their leadership boards that this exact margin collapse was imminent. We advised them that surviving this wasn't just a matter of changing the sales pitch; it required a ruthless rewiring of their entire talent architecture, from acquisition and mobility to development and retention.

The firms that listened made the hard choices early. They stopped hiring armies of network coordinators to boost vanity platform listings. Instead, they aggressively pivoted their acquisition strategy to poach hardcore supply chain operators, quality engineers, and execution-focused leaders who can control a factory floor they don't even own.

The result? While the rest of the market is currently bleeding capital trying to hire for a marketplace that mathematically no longer exists, the teams that executed this talent pivot built the exact operational foundation required to stabilize their margins. They are hitting their efficiency targets quarters ahead of schedule because they finally have the right operators driving the machine.

So, if your B2B model just introduces a buyer to a seller, your margins are trending toward zero. The next decade of industrial dominance belongs entirely to the operators who take full responsibility for the outcome.

They bought the software. They trusted the vendor. They never asked what it was actually doing.
 
Now someone is asking them.And the question is always the same.
How was this decision made?
Right now, in most organizations, the honest answer is:
The system decided. In seconds. And we trusted it.
That answer is not a defense. It's an admission.
Because here's what nobody told you when you bought the software — speed without documentation isn't efficiency. It's liability you haven't been invoiced for yet.
The ATS didn't file anything. The algorithm didn't sign anything. The AI didn't agree to produce records in discovery.
You did.
And when the investigation opens, they're not asking what tool you used. They're asking for the decision trail. The audit log. The bias assessment. The candidate notification records. The evidence that a human was actually in the loop.
Most organizations don't have it.
Not because they're malicious. Because they purchased a system, plugged it into their hiring process, and assumed they fell outside the definition of an Automated Employment Decision Tool.
They never investigated whether that assumption was correct. They never asked what the tool was actually doing to candidates. They never checked whether their use case triggered a compliance obligation.
They just trusted the vendor. And the vendor had a demo, a contract, and a very confident account manager.
And for multi-jurisdiction companies it compounds. A completed LL144 audit doesn't touch your EU AI Act obligations. A EU conformity assessment doesn't satisfy LL144. Having one doesn't cancel the other.
They thought compliance was a checkbox.
It was never a checkbox.
The machine was fast. The documentation wasn't there.
That gap — between the 11 seconds it took to reject a candidate and the evidence trail that never existed — is where the liability lives.
It doesn't matter which framework finds it first.
Have you audited what your hiring system is actually deciding?

New financial year. New challenges.

For many businesses, uncertainty has changed the equation.

AI is moving fast.
Budgets are tighter.
Permanent hiring decisions feel heavier.
And the long-term cost of today’s tooling choices is still far from settled.

That hesitation is understandable.

When conditions change, the old assumptions around hiring, investment, pace and risk change with them.

And while AI is influencing a lot of decision-making, not every AI-led move is automatically a good one. In many cases, organisations are still experimenting, still learning, and still trying to separate genuine value from market noise.

There is also a more practical question that does not get enough attention:
What happens when today’s attractive platform, tooling or AI pricing shifts tomorrow?

AI licensing costs will rise over time.

So before businesses go too far down the AI path, it is worth asking whether the long-term commercial model has really been thought through, or whether the current phase is still more experimentation than strategy.

At the same time, transformation is rarely just about buying new technology.
Operational complexity matters.

New platforms, cloud shifts, AI tooling, data flows, governance models, supplier dependencies, technical debt, delivery friction and commercial accountability all have to work together in the real world. That is often where progress slows, costs rise and confidence drops.

That is one reason why contract and fractional leadership can make a lot of sense in this market.

It gives businesses access to experience without forcing a long-term commitment too early.

It also creates space for targeted, time-boxed support around specific outcomes, whether that is shaping a new initiative, steadying delivery, navigating operational transformation, or helping bridge the gap between cloud-led innovation and the operational, commercial and delivery realities of telecoms.

For organisations working across cloud, telecoms and adjacent digital platforms, that can be the difference between momentum and drift.

I am open to conversations where experienced support is needed across solution architecture, technical delivery, growth leadership and technical sales.

Philips has had R&D engineers in Bangalore for years. Heineken opened a Hyderabad hub. Rabobank, TomTom, AkzoNobel, FrieslandCampina, Randstad all run their own capability centres in India.

For Dutch companies this is not new. What is changing is why it matters.
The early argument was cost. What these firms ended up with was capability and ownership. That turns out to matter more now that AI is on the table.

AI works best inside processes you actually control. Not because vendors cannot use AI, but because the real gains come from redesigning the process itself, and that is hard to do when the work sits under a fixed-scope contract with someone else.

The Dutch talent numbers are not getting easier either. Around 45,000 STEM graduates a year against 2.5 million in India. Hiring cycles of three to five months in Amsterdam against four to ten weeks in Hyderabad.

None of this makes a GCC the right answer for every company. It does make it a real question worth thinking through.

At Airopa Global the team has have put together a whitepaper on how Dutch companies can approach this. Worth a read if it is on your desk.

One in Five Unions Has AI Contract Protections. Here's What the Other Four Are Demanding in 2026.

Only 20% of unions worldwide have secured AI-specific provisions in collective bargaining agreements. But 42% are actively negotiating them right now.

That gap is the defining labor relations story of 2026 — and it's landing on HR's desk.

Here's what unions are demanding at the table:

→ Advance notice before AI tools are deployed that affect scheduling, performance reviews, or task allocation
→ Transparency about how algorithmic decisions are made — what data goes in, what triggers a flag
→ Right to a clear, human-readable explanation when AI directly affects a worker's conditions

These aren't theoretical. Fenway Park concession workers ratified an AI automation clause in December 2025. Spain's banking sector has AI provisions in sector-level agreements. The Colorado AI Act (effective June 2026) adds statutory pressure that unions can now use as a bargaining floor.

For HR leaders, three actions matter most before your next cycle:

1. Map every AI tool touching unionized roles — scheduling, evals, task allocation, hiring
2. Build internal disclosure protocols now, before you reach the table
3. Develop human-readable explanation templates for your highest-impact AI systems

The 20% of unions that already have AI protections are setting the terms for the next 20% — and the 20% after that.

If your AI screening tool systematically penalises CVs with employment gaps, you may be indirectly discriminating against women returning from maternity leave, people with long-term disabilities, and specific ethnic demographics. That's the warning the ICO effectively made in March 2026.

Here's what quietly changed in the legal backdrop in 2026, and why HR teams need to pay attention:

• The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 came fully into force on 5 February 2026. It replaced Article 22's blanket prohibition on automated decision-making with a legitimate-interests framework.

• The ICO's updated AI and Biometrics Strategy explicitly states they'll scrutinise the use of automated decision-making in recruitment in 2026, looking specifically at transparency, discrimination and redress.

• The ICO consultation on draft ADM guidance runs until 29 May 2026, with updated recruitment and selection guidance landing later this year.

• The Equality Act 2010 remains the sharpest risk. AI doesn't get a pass — if a tool produces a discriminatory outcome, the employer is liable.

The trap most SMBs walk into isn't malicious. It's this: you buy an AI screening tool, it works "well enough", and six months later you've made 80 hiring decisions with no documented human review, no Data Protection Impact Assessment, and no way to explain to a candidate why they were screened out.

A seven-point audit to run before the end of this quarter:

1. Map every AI touchpoint in your hiring funnel — including CV screening, video interview analysis, and chatbots
2. Run a DPIA before you deploy, not after an ICO letter arrives
3. Ask your vendor whose CVs the model was trained on
4. Define what "meaningful human review" actually looks like in your process
5. Write candidate-facing transparency notices
6. Test for adverse impact across protected characteristics
7. Set a retraining and monitoring cadence — at least quarterly

AI in recruitment is lawful in the UK. Careless AI in recruitment isn't. The difference is mostly documentation — and whether anyone has actually read the vendor contract.

Role: Principal AI Data Architect (AI-Ready Data Platform · ML/LLMOps · Agentic AI Infrastructure · Governance & Security )
Location: Remote
Exp :15+years Must have 
 
ABOUT THE ROLE 
We are hiring a Principal Data Architect — a hands-on, senior individual contributor who will design, build, govern, and evolve the single source of truth that powers every AI initiative in our organisation. This platform will serve as the foundational nervous system for conversational AI assistants, dashboard intelligence, autonomous AI agents, RAG-powered applications, predictive ML models, and any AI product we build today or in the future. 
You will architect the system, drive implementation, own the data contracts that agents and AI applications depend on, enforce security and access governance for both human and agent consumers, and continuously monitor and improve the accuracy and reliability of AI outputs that flow from this platform. You are the person who ensures our AI systems are only as good as the data beneath them — and you make that data exceptional. 

Please share resumes at sathvi@fusionplusinc.com
